overview: This is the most famous "Zionide" ever written. HaLevi composed it while living in Spain, consumed by Kissufim (burning longing) for the Land of Israel. The poem personifies Zion as a long-lost lover or mother, asking if she remembers her children scattered in exile. He wrote it to mobilize the spiritual heart of the Jewish people, eventually following his own words and traveling to the Land. Legend says he was killed by an Arab horseman while reciting these very lines at the gates of Jerusalem.
poem_lines: Zion, will you not ask the peace of your captives,
Those who seek your peace, the remnant of your flocks?
From West and East, from North and South—the peace
Of those far and near—enquire from all your sides.
Peace also to the captive of desire, who sheds his tears like dew
On Hermon, and longs to let them fall upon your hills.
To weep for your affliction, I am a jackal; but when I dream
Of your captives’ return—I am a harp for your songs.
My heart to Bethel and Pneil yearns intensely,
To Mahanaim and all the places where your pure ones met.
There the Presence dwells in you, and your Creator opened
Your gates to face the gates of Heaven.
The glory of the Lord alone was your light;
Neither sun nor moon nor stars gave light to you.
I would choose for my soul to be poured out in that place
Where the spirit of God was poured out upon your chosen ones.
You are the house of royalty, you are the throne of the Lord,
How then do slaves sit upon the thrones of your princes?
Who will give me wings that I might wander far away,
And carry the fragments of my heart to your mountains!
I will fall upon my face upon your land, I will cherish your stones
And I will be gracious to your very dust.
Life of souls is the air of your land, and of flowing myrrh
Is the dust of your ground, and of honeycomb your rivers.
It would be pleasant for my soul to walk naked and barefoot
Upon the desolate ruins where your shrines once stood.
In the place of your Ark, now hidden, and the place
Where your Cherubim dwelt in your inner chambers.
Shinar and Pathros—can they be compared to you in their greatness?
Can they compare their vanity to your Urim and Thummim?
To whom can they liken your anointed ones, and whom to your prophets,
And whom to your Levites and your singers?
The kingdom of idols shall pass and vanish, but your strength is forever,
Your crown is for all generations.
Your God has desired you for a dwelling place,
Happy is the man who chooses to draw near and dwell in your courts!
Happy is he who waits and reaches the time when he shall see
The rising of your light, and your dawns shall break upon him,
To see the goodness of your chosen ones, and to rejoice
In your joy, when you return to your former youth!
poem_hebrew_lines: וּשְׁלוֹם אֲסִיר תַּאֲוָה, נוֹתֵן דְּמָעָיו כְּטַל
לִבְכּוֹת עֱנוּתֵךְ אֲנִי תַנִּים, וְעֵת אֶחֱלֹם
לִבִּי לְבֵית אֵל וְלִפְנִיאֵל מְאֹד יִכְמַהּ,
וּכְבוֹד ה׳ לְבַד הָיָה מְאוֹרֵךְ, וְלֹא
אַתְּ בֵּית מְלוּכָה וְאַתְּ כִּסֵּא ה׳, וְאֵיךְ
מִי יַעֲשֶׂה לִי כְנָפַיִם וְאַרְחִיק נְדוֹד,
אֶפֹּל לְאַפַּי עֲלֵי אַרְצֵךְ וְאֶרְצֶה אֲבָנַיִךְ
חַיֵּי נְשָׁמוֹת אֲוִיר אַרְצֵךְ, וּמִמּוֹר דְּרוֹר
יִנעַם לְנַפְשִׁי הֲלֹךְ עָרֹם וְיָחֵף עֲלֵי
בִּמְקוֹם אֲרוֹנֵךְ אֲשֶׁר נִגְנַז, וּבִמְקוֹם אֲשֶׁר
עַל מִי יְדַמּוּ מְשִׁיחַיִךְ, וְעַל מִי נְבִיאֵיךְ,
